coredata.dc:title: Photoluminescence with an unusual open-loop and rigid delocalized conjugated structure in quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Xing, Hanli
Li, Xiaofang
Zhang, Susu
Li, Jun
Tang, Baokun
coredata.dc:description:
It is well known that almost all photoluminescent molecules are aromatic or heterocyclic ring compounds for bioimaging analysis. A question remains as to whether a breakthrough can be achieved regarding a novel photoluminescent molecule without a ring structure, and in a what manner. In this study, we explored the photoelectric conversion and structure of photoluminescent compounds, and constructed an intra-molecular coupling positive–negative-junction (PNJ) with an open-loop and rigid
Π
5
6
delocalized conjugated structure of the coupling p-π conjugate system. This was performed to enable strong absorption of the R/tail-end band for the high probability of an n → π*/n → σ* electron transition for photoluminescence production. Subsequently, the
Π
5
6
structure was formed in a short-chain aliphatic molecule as a hydrolytic product of citric acid and urea, and computational methodology was employed to estimate the feasibility of the molecule photoluminescence. Finally, a quantum dot material was fabricated from the aliphatic molecule, the optical properties of the quantum dots were investigated, and the biocompatibility and bioimaging ability of quantum dots were assessed. This work presents not only a theoretical exploration but also practical application of a new strategy to obtain molecules, compounds, and materials with bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bioimaging
Photoluminescence
PN junction
Delocalized conjugated structure
Quantum dot

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of temperature, solvent, time, and pH

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (E2018201155), Open Project Fund of Key Laboratory of Natural Resources of Changbai Mountain & Functional Molecules (Yanbian University), Ministry of Education (NRFM201705), and High-level Talents Introduction Program of Hebei University, and National Natural Science Foundation of China (81803287).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
(a) Construction of PNJ, (b) design of
Π
5
6
delocalized conjugated structure in the molecule, and (c) obtaining the target molecule by a dehydration reaction between CA and U.
(a) Chemical structure, (b) ΔE, and (c) HOMO and LUMO of the target molecule at S0, S1, and T1 based on Schrodinger Materials Science software calculation.
Identification of the molecular structure by (a) chromatography, (b) FTIR, (c) MS, and (d) 1H NMR and 13C NMR.
Characterization of QDs using (a, b) HRTEM, (c) Nano Measurer 1.2, (d) XRD, (e) X-ray mapping, and (f) EDS.

Images of (a) CA, U, and QD aqueous solutions under UV or without, (b) absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of QDs under UV, (c) emission spectra of QDs with different λex , (d) QY of QDs prepared in different DESs, and (e) SEM images of mixture obtained directly from reactions and from QD fabrication technology.
Effects of (a) preparation temperature, (b) solvent, (c) time, and (d) pH on QD photoluminescence.
Cell viability and morphology of (a) HeLa and (b) MCF-7 cells after incubation for 24 or 48 h with different concentrations of QDs.
(a) Photoluminescence of the QDs in different media under UV or without, (b) imaging of HeLa cells in control and QD samples, and (c) imaging of HeLa cells using QDs, DiO, and RedDotTM1.

head.ce:title: Photoluminescence with an unusual open-loop and rigid delocalized conjugated structure in quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Science, Institute of Life Science and Green Development, Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of Ministry of Education, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Pharmaceutical Science, Institute of Life Science and Green Development, Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of Ministry of Education, Hebei University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmaceutical Science, Institute of Life Science and Green Development, Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemistry and Molecular Diagnosis of Ministry of Education, Hebei University, Baoding 071002, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, North University of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
It is well known that almost all photoluminescent molecules are aromatic or heterocyclic ring compounds for bioimaging analysis. A question remains as to whether a breakthrough can be achieved regarding a novel photoluminescent molecule without a ring structure, and in a what manner. In this study, we explored the photoelectric conversion and structure of photoluminescent compounds, and constructed an intra-molecular coupling positive–negative-junction (PNJ) with an open-loop and rigid
Π
5
6
delocalized conjugated structure of the coupling p-π conjugate system. This was performed to enable strong absorption of the R/tail-end band for the high probability of an n → π*/n → σ* electron transition for photoluminescence production. Subsequently, the
Π
5
6
structure was formed in a short-chain aliphatic molecule as a hydrolytic product of citric acid and urea, and computational methodology was employed to estimate the feasibility of the molecule photoluminescence. Finally, a quantum dot material was fabricated from the aliphatic molecule, the optical properties of the quantum dots were investigated, and the biocompatibility and bioimaging ability of quantum dots were assessed. This work presents not only a theoretical exploration but also practical application of a new strategy to obtain molecules, compounds, and materials with bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic photoluminescent materials absorb a photon from ultraviolet or visible light, excite an electron to a highly excited state, and then radiate a photon to return to a lower ground state. Owing to the visualization enabled through photon radiation from a photoluminescent material, as well as other advantages such as low cost, eco-friendliness, and good biocompatibility,[1–3] such materials are popular in most scientific fields including biology, genomics, medicine, physics, and chemistry.[4–9] Considering the wider applications of organic photoluminescent materials, besides incorporation in conventional materials such as dyes, polymers, and proteins, their use in new materials such as rare-earth metal–organic chelates, dendritic polymers, nanoparticles, and aggregation-induced emission materials has been thoroughly explored.[10–13] An analysis of these photoluminescent materials at the molecular level and an overall survey of the molecular structures of the materials have shown that the structure of the molecule is key to photoluminescence. Such molecules clearly show an almost aromatic or heterocyclic ring (Figure S1). Further analysis reveals that an organic photoluminescent molecule should not only possess a large π-π conjugate and rigid plane bond structure, but also a smooth photoelectron transition pathway within the electron cloud structure.

Along with the conjugate structure and photoelectron transition pathway, we further explored the electron clouds and bond structures of a photoluminescent molecule based on acquired knowledge in the field of optics. Such a molecule generally features large π-π bonds with an electron cloud conjugate system, and easily exhibits strong K-band absorption under exposure to ultraviolet or visible light (Figure S2a). Consequently, the molecule has a large probability of undergoing π → π* electron transitions in an excited state (Figure S2b).[14] In addition, the molecules easily emit photons through radiation, releasing energy in the form of luminescence, as suggested by the El-Sayed rule and a Jablonski diagram (Figure S2c, S2d).[15] Based on this classical understanding, we attempted to achieve a breakthrough in the structure and electron transition mode of organic photoluminescent molecules.

For the above breakthrough, we first hoped to create a step during the photoelectron transition mode. In general, a positive–negative junction (PNJ) has been a popular choice to induce luminescence between P-type and N-type illuminants.[16–18] However, we attempted to establish an intra-molecular coupling PNJ for a photoelectron transition to achieve photoluminescence (Fig. 1 a). The step to construct the PNJ can be derived using a new and specific strategy. In this strategy, an electron in the p-orbit and the unsaturated electron area of a π-orbit can form a p-π non-π-π conjugate structure through an n → π* or n → σ* non-π → π* transition, thereby forming a weak R (radical) or tail-end absorption band to produce photoluminescence. From this perspective, the photoluminescence of an organic aliphatic molecule with a p-π conjugate structure, an n → π*/n → σ* transition, and an R/tail-end absorption band is our primary objective.

To achieve the above goal, three attempts should be made: (1) the conjugated system should be increased with an unusual p-π conjugation and without the common π-π conjugation for increasing the molar absorptivity, (2) the molecular structure should be kept rigid to maintain the above conjugated structure in a single plane,[19] and (3) spin–orbit coupling (SOC) should be promoted for producing photoluminescence,[20] while boosting intersystem crossing (ISC) to increase special phosphorescence (Figure S2d).[15] Among these, (2) and (3) are successfully accomplished with most π-π conjugated aromatic molecules,[15] but achieving a p-π conjugate system as required by (1) in an aliphatic molecule remains a significant challenge. Alternately, many conjugate π → π* transition systems in an aromatic molecule easily induce photoluminescence;[15,16] however, whether more than one n → π* and n → σ* transition conjugated in an aliphatic molecule can introduce photoluminescence has yet to be reported. Thus, using an aliphatic molecule, we hope to design a
Π
5
6
delocalized conjugated structure with a coupling p-π conjugation including n → π* and n → σ* transition modes, to increase the R and tail-end absorption for photoluminescence.

To successfully and efficiently obtain the target molecule and material, a new medium using deep eutectic solvents (DESs) and a new quantum dot (QD) fabrication technology were adopted. DESs are a new or analogous type of ionic liquid, and typically consist of a quaternary ammonium/phosphonium salt, such as a hydrogen bond acceptor (HBA), and a polyamine/polyol/polyacid acting as a hydrogen bond donor (HBD), which form in numerous hydrogen bond nets.[21] DESs have many excellent chemical and physical properties,[22] and, as a new medium [23] or functional reagent [24–26] , have been applied in different fields of science and found to help control the preparation of photoluminescent compounds.[27–29] Except for the choice of medium, QD fabrication as a nanotechnology has been proven to significantly increase the photoluminescence of compounds based on nanometer and quantum effects,5 and is therefore introduced as the technological base for the work presented herein.

With this in mind, to achieve a breakthrough regarding the limitations of the photoluminescence of organic molecules, we designed a short-chain aliphatic molecule with an intra-molecular coupling PNJ possessing an open-loop and rigid
Π
5
6
delocalized conjugated structure, prepared the molecule as a QD material, and evaluated the biocompatibility and bioimaging properties of the material.

Choline chloride (ChCl, 98%), citric acid (CA, 99.5%), histidine (His, 98.0%), phenylalanine (Phe, 98.0%), tyrosine (Tyr, 98.0%), aspartic acid (Asp, analytical reagent (AR)), tryptophan (Trp, AR), and glycine (Gly, biochemical reagent (BR)) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co. (Shanghai, China). Methyltriphenylphosphonium bromide (MB AR) was acquired from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ethylene glycol (EG, AR), glycerol (GL, AR), and lactic acid (LA, AR) were purchased from Tianjin Beichen Fangzheng Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Urea (U, 99.0%), malonic acid (MA, AR), ethanol (AR), methanol (AR), acetone (AR), tetrahydrofuran (THF, AR), chloroform (AR), petroleum ether (AR), n-hexane (AR), FeCl2 (AR), CoCl2 (AR), MgCl2 (AR), CaCl2 (AR), CuSO4 (AR), ZnSO4 (AR), NaNO3 (AR), NaCl (AR), KI (AR), KBr (AR), and HgCl2 (AR) were obtained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Anhydrous ferric chloride (FeCl3, AR) and quinine sulfate (98.0%) were purchased from Shanghai Meryer Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sucrose (Suc, AR), xylose (Xyl, AR), fructose (Fru, AR), glucose (Glc, AR), arabinose (Ara, AR), rhamnose (Rha, AR), galactose (Gal, AR), mannose (Man, AR), human serum albumin (HSA), a phosphate buffer (PBS, pH 7.0), 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetra-zolium bromide (MTT), Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM), and a DiO perchlorate membrane dye were obtained from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). β-Lactoglobulin (β-Lac, 90%) was acquired from Sigma Reagents Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, AR) was purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Finally, RedDot™1 nuclear dye was obtained from Biotium, Ltd. (California, USA).

A series of DESs were prepared through a typical heating process (Table S1):[30] a mixture of an HBA and an HBD at a specific molar ratio was heated to 95 °C from 10 min to 10 h, with constant stirring until a homogeneous liquid was obtained.

U and CA, at a molar ratio of 1:3, and the DESs were added together into 10 mL of deionized water in a 50 mL reaction kettle, and the mixtures were then heated at 160 °C for 4 h. To obtain the purified target compounds, a preparative chromatography system was used, consisting of LC-3000 binary high-pressure pumps and an SPD-UV3000-vis detector (Beijing Chuangxintongheng Science & Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). The separation was performed on a Venusil MP C18 column (5 μm, 100 Å, 250 × 10 mm i.d.). Chromatography-class methanol was used as the mobile phase with a flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1. Each solution was filtered (0.45 µm) before injection into the chromatography system. The injection volume was 60.0 μL and the wavelength of the UV detector was set to 210/333 nm.

In addition, the synthesis products were cooled to 20 ~ 30 °C and filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane to remove particles. The filtrate was then dialyzed using a membrane with a molecular weight cut-off of 500 for 24 h and freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain the QDs.

The recognition of the target molecules was performed by mass spectrometry (MS) (Q Exactive, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), 1H and 13C NMR spectroscopy (AVANCE III-600 MHz, Bruker, Switzerland), as well as Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (FTIR-8400S, Shimadzu, Japan). The morphology and structure of the QDs were characterized by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA). The optical properties were detected using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (T6, Puxi, China) and a fluorescence spectrometer (F970, Techcomp, China).

The quantum yield (QY) of the prepared materials was measured according to the established procedure. Briefly, quinine sulfate in 0.1 M of aqueous H2SO4 was chosen as a standard (a quantum yield of 55% has been described in the literature).[31] The samples were then dissolved in water, and the QY was calculated through the following equation:
(1)
Q Y
A
=
Q Y
R
(
I A
I R
)
(
η R
η A
)
2
where R is the standard, A is the sample, I is the slope of the integrated Em intensity versus Abs, and η is the refractive index of the solvent.

All simulations were conducted using the Schrodinger Materials Science software (Version 2019–2). The density functional theory (DFT), time-dependent DFT (TDDFT), and unrestricted DFT (UDFT) calculations were conducted using a B3LYP functional and a 6-31G* basis under a Jaguar module to obtain the ground state (S0) and excited state (S1 and T1) geometries, respectively. In addition, S0, S1, and T1 were optimized using the B3LYP functional and the 6-31G* basis under a QM Multistage Workflow to calculate the single-point energy, and the HOMO/LUMO (highest occupied molecular orbital/lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) frontier orbitals were then generated.

HeLa and MCF-7 cells were selected for the cytotoxicity test, and both were cultured in DMEM (including 1% penicillin–streptomycin) and Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) with a volume ratio of 9:1 at 37 °C in a humid atmosphere containing 5% CO2, respectively. Further, the cells were seeded in 96-well plates (2.0–5.5 × 104 cells per well) in DMEM and incubated within the same surroundings for 24 h. Subsequently, the DESs or QDs with DMEM/FBS were added into the 96-well plates for different culturing times, and 10 μL of MTT (5 mg mL−1) was added, with culturing continued for 4 h. Finally, the supernatant was removed, 100 μL of DMSO was added with shaking for 20 min, and the optical density (OD) was measured using a microplate reader at 490 nm. The cell viability was calculated using the following equation:
(2)
Cell
viability
(\%)
=
OD sample
-
OD
control
OD
n o r m a l
-
OD control
×
100 %
where ODsample is the absorbance of cells incubated with the QDs, ODcontrol is the absorbance of a blank medium without cells, and ODnormal is the absorbance of the blank medium with cells.

Based on an in vitro imaging investigation, HeLa cells were selected and cultured using the same processes as above; the cells were then mixed with the prepared quantum dots, and washed three times with PBS. Subsequently, the cells were directly imaged using an inverted microscope and confocal microscopy. For comparison, the cells were dyed using DiO perchlorate in the membrane and RedDot™1 in the nucleus.

In a photoelectron transition, the electron transfers from the enrichment zone to a hole passing through a PNJ, as shown in Fig. 1a.[16–18] Hence, constructing an intermolecular coupling PNJ can be an efficient approach for producing photoluminescence. We thus established that a p-π conjugate structure is suitable to construct a PNJ, because the negative electron in a p-orbit can transfer to the two unsaturated areas of the π orbits. However, to obtain a p-π conjugate structure for an n → π* or n → σ* electron transition based on an R or tail-end absorption band, carbonyl and heteroatoms can be easily and quickly chosen, and CO and N were considered as candidates for the conjugation (Fig. 1b). In addition, a N atom can not only offer a lone electron pair but also maintain the rigidity of its bonds by forming a tetrahedron framework (Fig. 1b).[19] To increase the p-π conjugate in delocalized systems, coupling p-π structures were designed for conjugation, and two CO groups and one N atom were conjugated to form a
Π
5
6
delocalized conjugation system (OC-NH-CO, see Fig. 1b). In a
Π
5
6
system, one carboxyl molecule and one amino molecule can be subjected to a dehydration reaction; therefore, U was selected for providing the amino molecules, CA was chosen for the carboxyl molecules, and ChCl/MB DESs were applied as the solvent media (Fig. 1c and Table S1).

A theoretical calculation is commonly conducted to study the feasibility and obtain deeper insights about the experimental phenomena.[14,32,33] As described through a Jablonski diagram (Figure S2d), the photoluminescence is closely related to the S0, S1, and T1 of an organic molecule. Fig. 2 a clearly shows the optimization structure of the target compound at S0, S1, and T1. In Fig. 2a, five atoms (OC-N-CO) of each
Π
5
6
system in the designed molecule at S0 form a dihedral angle at nearly 180.0° through bonding, which indicates that the atoms stay along a single plane with strong rigidity. The atoms of each
Π
5
6
system also remained in one plane even at S1 and T1, as shown with their dihedral angles close to 180°. For clear comparison of the three states, the molecular structures at S0, S1, and T1 are placed together, forming an exact overlap. This once more shows the co-planar and rigidity properties of the
Π
5
6
system at any state of the designed molecule, as is necessary for photoluminescence attempt (2) described in the Introduction.

Except for an analysis of the structure of the
Π
5
6
system, the energy change (ΔE) of the designed molecules at different states of S0, S1, and T1, is also critical, and was calculated as shown in Fig. 2b. The S0 energy of the designed molecule was calculated as 0.0H (Hartree, the potential energy of one hydrogen atom at the ground state, 1.0H = 2,625.5 kJ mol−1 = 27.2 eV), and the S1 and T1 energies were 0.144H and 0.127H, respectively. The ΔE of S1–S0 (0.144H) is sufficiently large to produce nonradiative fluorescence, and a value of 0.018H for a low ΔEST (T1–S1) suppresses the ISC without phosphorescence,[34] which correlates with photoluminescence attempt (3).

To further explore the photoluminescent nature of the
Π
5
6
system, the electron cloud changes of the system at S0, S1, and T1 in the molecule were characterized through a molecular orbital calculation and are depicted in Fig. 2c. Clearly, the
Π
5
6
system (OC-N-CO) is almost at the center of the electron cloud at the HOMO and LUMO, which corresponds to the designed
Π
5
6
system. Only the electron cloud center transfers from one
Π
5
6
system to another in the molecule between the HOMO and LUMO transitions. In addition, the energy gaps (ΔE) between the LUMO and HOMO of S0, S1, and T1 are 0.219H, 0.131H, and 0.146H, respectively, which correspond to the stability order of S0 > T1 > S1. Thus, the lowest ΔE of S1 promotes SOC for fluorescence, and the ΔE of T1 suppresses ISC for phosphorescence more than that of S1. The calculation was a response to attempt (1) and shows good agreement with the test results, as presented subsequently.
To confirm the structural characteristics of our designed compound at the molecular level, the results of the chromatography, FTIR, MS, 1H, and 13C NMR analyses were analyzed (Fig. 3 ).

To obtain the purified target compound, a C18 reversed-phase preparative column with a UV–vis detector was used to separate the material with two detection wavelengths at 210/330 nm. In the chromatogram of Fig. 3a, the product peak is apparent at 11.3 min at 330 nm, corresponding to the maximum absorption wavelength in the UV spectrum in Section 3.4.1. The product was collected at the peak absorption with a tube for subsequent characterization.

In the FTIR spectra (Fig. 3b), the peaks of the product are clearly different from those of the reactants; the peak of 3,250 cm−1 is attributed to the N H stretching vibration by a wide and deep absorption, and peaks at 3,020 and 2,950 cm−1 are ascribed to the O H and N H vibrations of the amide groups in the designed molecules, respectively. Moreover, the peak at 1,696 cm−1 is assigned to the CO stretching vibration absorption, and the peak at 1,594 cm−1 corresponds to the in-plane bending vibration absorption of N H.[19] These results of FTIR confirm the presence of the amide functional groups of the designed molecule.

In addition, the designed molecule (M with m/z = 318) as a whole is identical based on the ESI-MS spectrum with a positive mode (Fig. 3c). Further, the purified target compound with m/z = 318.29 (M+) and 319.30 (M + H+) ions was detected, which confirms the presence of the designed molecule.

To further confirm the molecular structure of the designed molecule, the 1H and 13C NMR spectra are shown in Fig. 3d. There are clearly no peaks (greater than9.0 ppm) of the CA carboxyl protons in the 1H spectra, which implies that the carboxyls of CA had reacted with the aminos of U to generate amide bonds in the product. In addition, the highest peak at 4.72 ppm arises from the proton of the only hydroxyl of the designed molecule, the peak at 3.32 ppm is attributed to the protons of the imino group, and the peak at 3.15 ppm is ascribed to the protons on the primary amine group. In the 13C NMR spectra, the peaks at 58 and 54 ppm are respectively attributed to the carbons of the external and central amide bonds. The peak at 49 ppm is assigned to the carbon adjacent to the hydroxyl group, and the peaks at 17 ppm are due to the methylene carbons. The above analysis supports the existence of the molecular structure of the designed molecule.

The overall characteristics of the material are also important to its physical/chemical properties. Therefore, the prepared material was characterized by HRTEM, XRD, X-ray mapping, and EDS (Fig. 4 ). The zoomed-out HRTEM image in Fig. 4a clearly shows that the prepared materials were spherical and well dispersed without agglomeration, and the zoomed-in image in Fig. 4b reveals a fine lattice layer of thickness 0.21 nm. In addition, the diameters of the particles ranged from 1.2 to 4.8 nm with most near 2.5 nm (Fig. 4c), which is within the essential size requirement of 10 nm for QDs.[35] Therefore, we can refer to the prepared materials as QDs hereafter. Furthermore, the XRD data (Fig. 4d), with one lone peak at 2θ = 25.8°, support the illustration of QDs as crystals. The coupled mapping (Fig. 4e) and EDX (Fig. 4f) modes with HRTEM show key elements of C, O, N, and Cl in the QDs, confirming that the designed materials were obtained from raw reagents.

In comparison with the CA and U raw reagents, the prepared QDs in water under ambient conditions with UV light (365 nm) clearly exhibited photoluminescence that was visible to the naked eye, whereas the raw reagents showed no luminescence (Fig. 5 a). The Abs (absorption), Ex (excitation), and Em (emission) spectra of the prepared QDs are shown in Fig. 5b. The end absorption peak suggests the n → σ* transition of the CN bonds, and the two peaks at λ (wavelength) of 234 and 335 nm correspond respectively to the π → π* and n → π* transitions of the CO bonds with K and R absorption bands, similar to previous studies.[5,36] In addition, the p-π conjugation between the lone pair of electrons of –N and the electrons of the π bonds in –CO promoted a red shift and hyperchromic effect in the K and R bands. This hyperchromic effect indicates an increase in the transition probability of π → π* and n → π* to facilitate the generation of photoluminescence.[5,34,37] The Ex and Em spectra were obtained based on the Abs spectrum, with the λ of Ex close to 330 nm, while Em shows a Stokes shift. Thus, the above spectra of the designed compound or prepared material can be attributed to the quantum effects.[38]

Except for the base property, the optical properties of our QDs were detected as described above: the λ of Ex (λex ) was investigated to be within the range of 310–400 nm (Fig. 5c), and 353 nm was deduced to be the maximum value of λex .

In addition, the photoluminescence of the QDs was strongly dependent on the preparation conditions,[39,40] and a series of DESs (Table S1) were investigated for their effects on the photoluminescence of the corresponding QDs, the results of which are shown in Fig. 5d. Each QY of the QDs prepared in the DESs was more than the control QY (1.4%), which indicates that the DESs were not only an eco-friendly medium for preparing the QDs, but also assisted with the luminescence, which is in good agreement with Wang's study.[27] In addition, when investigating the series of DESs in detail, most of the QYs of the QDs with ChCl DESs (DES-1, 2, 3, and 9, excluding 4) were found to be greater than those of the MB DESs (DES-5, 6, 7, and 8). This occurred because ChCl prefers to hydrate with water to facilitate a dehydration reaction (Fig. 1a), and ChCl also affords N heteroatoms as precursors and surface passivation agents for increasing the luminescence effect.[34] Based on these results, we selected the common ChCl-U DES-9 as the medium; this, together with the fact that most of the QYs were 16.2%, led to distinct luminescence intensity in comparison to the control shown in Fig. 5d. Further, to achieve quantum effects by controlling the size of the QDs,[35] a dialysis bag was used to achieve homogeneity with a size of less than 10.0 nm (Fig. 5e).
These results verify that the quantum and DES effects can efficiently enhance the optical properties of the designed compounds and materials described herein.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of temperature, solvent, time, and pH
ce:section.ce:para: For an overall investigation of the factors influencing the optical properties of the QDs, the drying treatment temperature was changed from −48 to 400 °C. At such temperatures, the prepared materials were observed to have a carbonization form and were insoluble at a temperature higher than 275 °C. Therefore, carbonization clearly destroyed the structure of the materials,[41,42] and the luminescence phenomena were significantly weakened with an increase in temperature under UV (Fig. 6 a), which is similar to the report by Giannelis et al.[20] To evaluate the effect of the solvent on the solubility and luminescence property of the QDs, conventional polar (water, methanol, ethanol, and DMSO) and nonpolar (acetone, THF, chloroform, petroleum ether, and n-hexane) solvents were selected; the QDs easily dissolved in the polar solvents, exhibiting a uniform luminescence (Fig. 6b). Fig. 6c illustrates a favorable stability of the luminescence of the QDs, with no apparent variation after 80 days. The pH is also a sensitive factor in the luminescence of nanoparticles;[19,43] therefore, the luminescence intensity of the QDs was determined by varying the pH within a range of 2.0–12.0 (Fig. 6d). Herein, the luminescence intensity increased with a pH of more than 5.0, but decreased with a pH less than 4.0. Thus, a marked jump occurred between a pH of 4.0 and 5.0, which indicates that the pH can change the molecular structure of the QDs, and that the QDs can be an indicator of the pH.[19] The above detailed investigation will be favorable for choosing the environmental conditions in the photoluminescence application of the QDs. Further, the clear optical properties make the QDs promising for more functional applications.

When developing green applications, the biocompatibility of newly prepared materials requires serious consideration.[3] Therefore, cytotoxicity tests of the QDs were conducted through an MTT assay on HeLa and MCF-7 cell lines, the morphologies of which were observed using an inverted microscope (Fig. 7 ). The viability and morphology of the HeLa cells were investigated at different concentrations (100–600 μg mL−1) of the QDs for different time periods (24 and 48 h) (Fig. 7a). Most HeLa cells exhibited a viability more than 80% with morphological integrality, but slightly decreased viability with increasing concentration or time. Similar results were obtained with MCF-7 (Fig. 7b), i.e., a high viability. The integral morphology of MCF-7 with the QDs exhibited similar effects as those of the HeLa cells. The reactants CA and U are popular biocompatible compounds,[6,19] while the DESs are also natural and benign media.[44,45] Hence, the QD products from both have a natural biocompatibility that is in good agreement with the above result. In addition, the positive charge of the amino groups on the QDs easily interacts with the negative charge of the cell membrane for internalization.[46] Thus, the QDs were confirmed as being highly biocompatible for use in potential green applications.

Based on their outstanding optical properties and biocompatibility, the QDs can be successfully applied in the bioimaging of cells. Further, the luminescence of the QDs can be easily transferred from pure water to PBS, FBS, and even the culture medium, and efficiently avoid interference from other luminescent sources. In addition, the intensity (I/I0) in each of the solutions did not clearly change (Fig. 8 a). We employed the luminescence of the QDs in the bioimaging of HeLa cells. HeLa cells incubated with QDs in the medium became brighter under a bright field, and exhibited an apparent blue emission upon excitation at 355 nm; in contrast, no visible luminescence was detected in the control cells under the same condition (Fig. 8b). Based on these results, the cells were clearly numbered under a confocal microscope.

However, cells usually need to be labeled in multiple colors with different dyes for distinguishing the cell components such as the membrane, nucleus, mitochondria, DNA, RNA, and other components.[47–49] Although the distinct bioimaging function of the QDs allows direct tracing of the cells, whether they can compatibly and clearly image cells with other bioimaging probes remains to be investigated. Therefore, two popular probes, i.e., DiO for labeling a membrane[48] and RedDotTM1 for the nucleus,[50] were selected for use with the QDs in the culturing and imaging of HeLa cells. In Fig. 8c, DiO and RedDotTM1 clearly show a cell membrane with green color at an Ex wavelength of 488 nm, and a nucleus with red color at 559 nm in the control groups. The QDs were successfully cultured in HeLa cells with both of the above bio-probes, and could clearly image the multiple components of the cell membrane at 355 nm, even with the two bio-probes shown at the bottom of Fig. 8. Herein, the QDs not only showed the peripheral membrane of the cells through QD biocompatibility, but also showed the internal nucleus, nucleolus, and chromatin of the cells through endocytosis.[27,46,51,52] These results revealed the high imaging capability and compatibility of QDs as an ideal cell bio-probe.

A novel photoluminescent molecule of a short aliphatic chain with an intra-molecular PNJ in an open-loop and rigid
Π
5
6
delocalized conjugated structure was elaborately designed and successfully applied in bioimaging. The designed molecule was obtained in the photoelectron transition mode (through n → π* and n → σ* transitions), and its chemical structure (without a ring) was significantly different from those of other popular photoluminescent molecules. Further, the developed molecules were prepared as a QD material using DES media and nanotechnology. The prepared QDs showed both basic and excellent optical properties in different ambient environments. Based on the optical properties, the QDs exhibited good biocompatibility with the cells; clear imaging was achieved of the internal nucleus, nucleolus, and chromatin of the cells. Overall, this study offers a new developmental direction for organic photoluminescent molecules or materials in bioimaging, based on the theoretical exploration and practical application provided herein.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (E2018201155), Open Project Fund of Key Laboratory of Natural Resources of Changbai Mountain & Functional Molecules (Yanbian University), Ministry of Education (NRFM201705), and High-level Talents Introduction Program of Hebei University, and National Natural Science Foundation of China (81803287).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.05.112.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
